verses.text: Now Hiram king of Tyre sent messengers to David, and timber of cedars, with masons and carpenters, to build him an house. And David perceived that the LORD had confirmed him king over Israel, for his kingdom was lifted up on high, because of his people Israel. Guidance: Hiram of Tyre sends cedars and craftsmen to build David a house, and David, far from being puffed up, draws a humble and godly conclusion: 'David perceived that the LORD had confirmed him king over Israel, for his kingdom was lifted up on high, because of his people Israel.' He read his rising fortunes rightly — as the LORD's doing, not his own achievement — and he understood the purpose of his exaltation: it was 'because of his people Israel,' for their sake, not merely for his own glory. The chapter is showing us a king who interprets his success spiritually, tracing every advancement to the hand of God. The chapter is teaching us the grace of perceiving God's hand in our prosperity: when honour and provision come (even from a foreign king like Hiram), the godly heart says, 'the LORD has done this,' and is humbled rather than exalted. The chapter is teaching us, too, that the LORD lifts up His servants not for their own sake but for the sake of His people — David's throne was raised high so that Israel might be blessed, governed, and led toward God. Whatever position or influence the LORD grants us is a trust for the good of others, not a trophy for ourselves. The chapter is teaching us to receive blessing with humility and to hold every elevation as a stewardship 'because of' the people God has given us to serve. David's clear-eyed humility here stands in beautiful contrast to the pride that ruins so many who rise to power; he knew Who had established him, and why.

And David took more wives at Jerusalem: and David begat more sons and daughters. Now these are the names of his children which he had in Jerusalem; Shammua, and Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon, And Ibhar, and Elishua, and Elpalet, And Nogah, and Nepheg, and Japhia, And Elishama, and Beeliada, and Eliphalet. Guidance: The Chronicler records David's growing household at Jerusalem — more wives, and many sons and daughters, among them Nathan and Solomon, the two through whose lines (Luke and Matthew respectively) the Messiah would come. The note that 'David took more wives' is recorded plainly, without comment, yet the discerning reader hears a quiet warning, for the LORD had forbidden Israel's king to 'multiply wives to himself' (Deut 17:17), and David's many wives would breed much of the strife and sorrow of his later years. The chapter is showing us both the LORD's blessing on David's house in the gift of children and the seed of future trouble in David's departure from God's design for marriage. The chapter is teaching us that even a man after God's own heart had his besetting weaknesses, and that the blessings of God (a large family, a flourishing house) can be mingled with the consequences of our own disobedience. The chapter is teaching us not to presume that prosperity equals full obedience; David grew great and yet here quietly transgressed a clear command. Yet grace shines even here: out of this very house, through Nathan and Solomon, the LORD would bring the Christ. The chapter is teaching us that God's redemptive purposes run on through flawed families and imperfect saints — He wove the line of the Messiah through David's children, mingling sovereign grace with human frailty. We are taught both to take warning from David's compromise and to marvel that the LORD brings His greatest gift, the Savior, through a lineage so marked by human weakness.

And when the Philistines heard that David was anointed king over all Israel, all the Philistines went up to seek David. And David heard of it, and went out against them. And the Philistines came and spread themselves in the valley of Rephaim. And David enquired of God, saying, Shall I go up against the Philistines? and wilt thou deliver them into mine hand? And the LORD said unto him, Go up; for I will deliver them into thine hand. So they came up to Baalperazim; and David smote them there. Then David said, God hath broken in upon mine enemies by mine hand like the breaking forth of waters: therefore they called the name of that place Baalperazim. And when they had left their gods there, David gave a commandment, and they were burned with fire. Guidance: When the Philistines come up against the newly-crowned king, David does the very thing Saul had failed to do — he 'enquired of God': 'Shall I go up against the Philistines? and wilt thou deliver them into mine hand?' The LORD answers, David obeys, and the victory is complete; David names the place Baal-perazim, 'the place of breakings-through,' confessing 'God hath broken in upon mine enemies by mine hand like the breaking forth of waters.' The chapter is showing us the great mark of David's life and the antidote to Saul's ruin: David enquired of God before he acted. He did not presume on his own strength or his royal anointing; he asked, he waited for God's word, and then he went. The chapter is teaching us to seek the LORD's guidance before our undertakings, especially our battles — to ask, 'Shall I go? wilt thou deliver?' rather than rushing forward in self-confidence. The chapter is teaching us that the victory belongs to God: David gave Him all the glory — 'God hath broken in upon mine enemies by mine hand,' acknowledging that his hand was merely the instrument and God the true conqueror, breaking through like a flood. The chapter is teaching us, in the burning of the Philistines' abandoned idols, the proper response to false gods: no compromise, no curiosity, but utter destruction. Where Israel had once carried God's ark out to battle like a charm and lost it to these same Philistines (1 Sam 4), David now triumphs by seeking the living God and burns the dead idols of his foes. The chapter is teaching us to enquire of God, to obey His word, to ascribe the victory to Him, and to make no peace with the idols He has overthrown.

And the Philistines yet again spread themselves abroad in the valley. Therefore David enquired again of God; and God said unto him, Go not up after them; turn away from them, and come upon them over against the mulberry trees. And it shall be, when thou shalt hear a sound of going in the tops of the mulberry trees, that then thou shalt go out to battle: for God is gone forth before thee to smite the host of the Philistines. David therefore did as God commanded him: and they smote the host of the Philistines from Gibeon even to Gazer. And the fame of David went out into all lands; and the LORD brought the fear of him upon all nations. Guidance: When the Philistines return and spread out in the valley a second time, David does not presume that what worked before will work again — 'David enquired again of God.' And this time God gives a different command: do not attack head-on, but circle behind to the mulberry trees, and wait for 'a sound of going in the tops of the mulberry trees,' the sign that 'God is gone forth before thee to smite the host of the Philistines.' David obeys exactly, and wins a great victory, his fame spreading to all lands. The chapter is showing us the wisdom of seeking God afresh for each new situation; David did not lean on yesterday's guidance or assume the same strategy would serve, but enquired again — and God's answer was different. The chapter is teaching us not to presume upon past experience or to fall into routine in our walk with God; what He blessed once He may direct differently the next time, and we must keep asking, keep listening, keep depending. The chapter is teaching us the patience of faith: David had to wait for the appointed sign, the sound in the treetops, before he moved — acting not by his own timing but by God's signal, for 'God is gone forth before thee.' The chapter is teaching us the deep comfort that God goes before His people into their battles; the rustling in the mulberry trees was the sound of the LORD Himself marching out ahead to smite the enemy, so that David followed where God had already gone. The chapter is teaching us to enquire repeatedly, to obey precisely, to wait for God's timing, and to take courage that the LORD goes before us. And the spreading of David's fame and 'the fear of him upon all nations' shows that when a man walks in such dependent obedience, God exalts him and works through him for His own glory among the nations — a foretaste of the kingdom of David's Son, whose name shall be feared and honoured in all the earth.

David obeys exactly, and wins a great victory, his fame spreading to all lands. The chapter is showing us the wisdom of seeking God afresh for each new situation; David did not lean on yesterday's guidance or assume the same strategy would serve, but enquired again — and God's answer was different. The chapter is teaching us not to presume upon past experience or to fall into routine in our walk with God; what He blessed once He may direct differently the next time, and we must keep asking, keep listening, keep depending. The chapter is teaching us the patience of faith: David had to wait for the appointed sign, the sound in the treetops, before he moved — acting not by his own timing but by God's signal, for 'God is gone forth before thee.' The chapter is teaching us the deep comfort that God goes before His people into their battles; the rustling in the mulberry trees was the sound of the LORD Himself marching out ahead to smite the enemy, so that David followed where God had already gone. The chapter is teaching us to enquire repeatedly, to obey precisely, to wait for God's timing, and to take courage that the LORD goes before us. And the spreading of David's fame and 'the fear of him upon all nations' shows that when a man walks in such dependent obedience, God exalts him and works through him for His own glory among the nations — a foretaste of the kingdom of David's Son, whose name shall be feared and honoured in all the earth.

And the Philistines yet again spread themselves abroad in the valley. Therefore David enquired again of God; and God said unto him, Go not up after them; turn away from them, and come upon them over against the mulberry trees. And it shall be, when thou shalt hear a sound of going in the tops of the mulberry trees, that then thou shalt go out to battle: for God is gone forth before thee to smite the host of the Philistines. David therefore did as God commanded him: and they smote the host of the Philistines from Gibeon even to Gazer. And the fame of David went out into all lands; and the LORD brought the fear of him upon all nations. Guidance: When the Philistines return and spread out in the valley a second time, David does not presume that what worked before will work again — 'David enquired again of God.' And this time God gives a different command: do not attack head-on, but circle behind to the mulberry trees, and wait for 'a sound of going in the tops of the mulberry trees,' the sign that 'God is gone forth before thee to smite the host of the Philistines.' David obeys exactly, and wins a great victory, his fame spreading to all lands. The chapter is showing us the wisdom of seeking God afresh for each new situation; David did not lean on yesterday's guidance or assume the same strategy would serve, but enquired again — and God's answer was different. The chapter is teaching us not to presume upon past experience or to fall into routine in our walk with God; what He blessed once He may direct differently the next time, and we must keep asking, keep listening, keep depending. The chapter is teaching us the patience of faith: David had to wait for the appointed sign, the sound in the treetops, before he moved — acting not by his own timing but by God's signal, for 'God is gone forth before thee.' The chapter is teaching us the deep comfort that God goes before His people into their battles; the rustling in the mulberry trees was the sound of the LORD Himself marching out ahead to smite the enemy, so that David followed where God had already gone. The chapter is teaching us to enquire repeatedly, to obey precisely, to wait for God's timing, and to take courage that the LORD goes before us. And the spreading of David's fame and 'the fear of him upon all nations' shows that when a man walks in such dependent obedience, God exalts him and works through him for His own glory among the nations — a foretaste of the kingdom of David's Son, whose name shall be feared and honoured in all the earth.
